title: Switchable Load Control
learningObjectives: Build a switched series loop for safe on/off control
Set current limits before closing the switch
Observe how switch state affects circuit validity
introText: Switches add control to simple circuits. You'll wire a series loop with a switch, set safe limits, and confirm the circuit only evaluates when closed.

steps.instructionText: Place a DC source, resistor, and switch in series, then wire the loop closed.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Loop is closed. The switch is part of the series path.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Connect all three components end-to-end. Both switch terminals must be wired.

steps.instructionText: Set the source near 9V and resistor around 470Ω to keep current below 20 mA.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Current is under 20 mA — safe for many indicator circuits.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Raise the resistor value or lower the voltage until current drops below 20 mA.

steps.instructionText: Toggle the switch property if needed and re-check that the circuit stays valid with safe current.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Current remains under 20 mA after toggling — your settings are robust.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: If current rises, increase resistance or lower the voltage again.

questions.questionText: What should you verify before closing a switch in a live circuit?
options: Total resistance and expected current are within limits
questions.explanation: Set safe resistance and voltage first so current stays within limits when the switch closes.

questions.questionText: If a switch opens the loop, what happens in this simulator?
options: The circuit becomes invalid because the loop breaks
questions.explanation: Opening the loop breaks connectivity, making the circuit invalid until rewired/closed.
